course_description:
Software Defined Networking, Network Programming, Peer-to-peer Networks, Network Management, Queuing Theory, Wireless Networks

This course will give an introduction into a number of advanced networking concepts, including Software Defined Networking (SDN),ﾠPeer-to-peer Networks, Network Management, Queuing Theory, and aspects ofﾠWireless Networks.

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:
incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
course_aims: The course will give an introduction into Software Defined Networking (SDN). In team-basedﾠassignments, students will explore advanced networking concepts, e.g. by designing and implementing an SDN application. The course further covers a range of advanced Computer Networking topics such as: Network Management, Queuing Theory, Peer-to-peer Computing,ﾠﾠaspects of Wireless Networksﾠand in particular IoT Networks.

learning_outcomes.description: Design and implement Networking (SDN-based or other) applications to implement a range of networking functionalities and services.
Demonstrate the application of Network Management concepts to practical computer networking scenarios.
Analyse and evaluate performance aspects of computer networks using queuing theory.
Apply peer-to-peer computing concepts to solve distributed computing problems.
Communicate with peers and work efficiently in a small team.
Analyse computer networking problems, and formulate and implement practical solutions, individually as well as in a small team.
Analyse and evaluate the functional and performance aspects of computer networks, including Software Defined Networks, using network emulation as an experimental platform.

assessment_details.title: Project: Part A
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Students (in small teams) are required to undertake a research/implementation project on an advanced networking topic, e.g. SDN. This assessment item includes a project proposal and progress report. Use of AI Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly and specifically reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Maximum Extension: 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours. Marked assignments with feedback and/or detailed solutions with feedback will be released to students within 14-21 days
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Project: Part B - Presentation
assessment_details.due_date: 20/10/2025 - 31/10/2025 In Semester Week 12 or 13, at a time scheduled with the course coordinator
assessment_details.task_description: A presentation/demo/interview (in Semester Week 12 or 13, at a time scheduled with the course coordinator) In the Presentation/Interview/Demo component of the Project Part B assessment, teams will provide a verbal presentation of their project, with visual support (slides, etc.). The duration of the presentation should be around 10 minutes. If the project includes an implementation component, a demo of the implemented system will be provided. This is then followed by a Q&A session. Use of AI This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Extensions for groupwork are typically not available as this impacts on all members of the team.
Students with valid extension requests to receive team mark or be required to undertake alternative assessment.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
Consistent with industry practice around presentations to clients/industry partners, the 100% late penalty applies.

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Lectures Lectures in COMS6200 are designed to deliver the core theoretical content of the course. Typically held for 2 hours per week, these sessions introduce students to advanced concepts in computer networking, such as Software Defined Networking, Queuing Theory, and Wireless Networks. Lectures are led by the course coordinator and/or a lecturer and cover the foundational knowledge and principles required to understand complex network systems. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Applied Class Applied Classes, are 1-hour weekly sessions that provide a smaller, interactive setting for students to deepen their understanding of lecture material. These sessions focus on applying theoretical concepts through problem-solving, discussions, and exercises. Applied classes often involve practice questions and are a chance to get feedback, clarify doubts, discuss upcoming assessments, and engage with peers. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04

Practicals Practicals, are weekly hands-on classes where students apply theoretical knowledge to practical scenarios, in a computer lab environment. These sessions focus on developing technical skills, such as configuring Software Defined Network systems, or programming network applications. Students work individually, using lab facilities or, in some cases, personal computing equipment. These sessions are crucial for gaining experience with real-world Software Defined Networking tools and techniques. Learning outcomes: L01, L06, L07
